Here are skills that are generally useful and especially relevant for working in Materialize.

---

## High-value skills to define

### 1. **Project / codebase conventions (project skill)**  
**Path:** `.cursor/skills/materialize-conventions/` (in the repo)

- **Purpose:** How this codebase is structured and how to work in it.
- **Include:** Rust crate layout (`src/`, `mz_*` vs internal crates), where SQL/parser vs execution lives, how to run tests (`cargo test`, testdrive), and any doc/developer guides.
- **Triggers:** “How do I…”, “Where is…”, “Add a new…”, “Run tests for…”

This keeps the agent from guessing and makes edits match project style.

### 2. **Commit message / PR conventions (personal or project)**  
**Path:** Personal `~/.cursor/skills/` or project `.cursor/skills/`

- **Purpose:** Generate commit messages and PR descriptions in your team’s format.
- **Include:** Conventional-commit style if you use it (e.g. `fix(expr):`, `feat(sql):`), scope conventions (crate or area), and a short template for PR descriptions.
- **Triggers:** “Write a commit message”, “Summarize this PR”, “Draft PR description”.

### 3. **Rust-in-this-repo (project skill)**  
**Path:** `.cursor/skills/materialize-rust/`

- **Purpose:** Rust patterns and constraints specific to Materialize.
- **Include:** Use of `mz_*`/`ore`/`repr`/`expr` where relevant, error handling (e.g. `anyhow`/`thiserror`), async style, and “don’t do X here” (e.g. avoid certain std APIs if you have replacements).
- **Triggers:** “Implement in Rust”, “Refactor this”, “Add error handling”, “Optimize this”.

### 4. **SQL / catalog changes (project skill)**  
**Path:** `.cursor/skills/materialize-sql-catalog/`

- **Purpose:** How SQL features and catalog objects are added or changed.
- **Include:** Where parser, planning, and catalog code live; how new types/functions/operators are wired (e.g. `pgrepr`, `repr`, SQL layer); and that catalog changes often need migrations or version bumps.
- **Triggers:** “Add SQL support for…”, “New scalar type”, “New function”, “Catalog change”.

### 5. **Testing (testdrive, sqllogictest, unit tests) (project skill)**  
**Path:** `.cursor/skills/materialize-testing/`

- **Purpose:** How to add and run tests for SQL, APIs, and pipelines.
- **Include:** testdrive syntax and location, sqllogictest, where unit tests live, and how to run the relevant test suite (e.g. `./mzcompose run testdrive …` or `cargo test -p …`).
- **Triggers:** “Add a test”, “Run testdrive”, “Reproduce this bug”, “Test this SQL”.

### 6. **Code review (personal or project)**  
**Path:** Personal or project

- **Purpose:** Review PRs and patches in a consistent way.
- **Include:** Checklist (correctness, safety, performance, style), how to comment (e.g. “blocking” vs “nit”), and pointers to CONTRIBUTING or internal review docs.
- **Triggers:** “Review this PR”, “Review my changes”, “Check this diff”.

### 7. **Debugging / investigation (personal or project)**  
**Path:** Personal `~/.cursor/skills/` or project

- **Purpose:** Standard way to narrow down bugs (e.g. SQL wrong result, panic, perf regression).
- **Include:** How to get logs, run under a debugger, use testdrive to isolate, and where common failure modes live (parser, planning, execution, storage).
- **Triggers:** “Debug this”, “Why does this fail”, “Investigate this error”.

---

## How to choose

| If you want… | Start with… |
|--------------|--------------|
| The agent to “get” the repo | **Materialize conventions** |
| Consistent commits/PRs | **Commit / PR conventions** |
| Safe Rust and SQL changes | **Rust-in-this-repo** + **SQL/catalog** |
| Fewer test mistakes | **Testing** |
| Consistent reviews | **Code review** |
| Faster bug hunting | **Debugging** |

Start with **one or two** (e.g. conventions + testing or conventions + Rust), then add more as you see repeated questions or mistakes. Keep each skill’s **description** specific and in third person (“Use when…”) so the agent reliably picks it. If you tell me which one you want first (e.g. “Materialize conventions” or “commit messages”), I can draft the `SKILL.md` structure and description for it using the create-skill format.

```
[33mcommit f79fc1c82e9db6aafca8409d2b7761c0469c3c3b[m
Author: mtabebe &lt;michael.abebe@materialize.com&gt;
Date:   Wed Feb 18 13:41:21 2026 -0500

    [10089] [Adapter] Fix COPY panic on invalid range by making into_datum return Result
    
    Problem:
    into_datum had no way to report errors, so COPY with a malformed range
    (e.g. [7,3) with lower &gt; upper) caused a panic instead of a user-visible error.
    
    Solution:
    - Introduce IntoDatumError (wrapping InvalidRangeError and InvalidArrayError)
    and make Value::into_datum return Result&lt;Datum, IntoDatumError&gt;.
    - Add into_datum_decode_error for decode/parameter contexts with a consistent
    error message
    - Propagate the error at all call sites (pgcopy, pgwire bind, environmentd
    HTTP SQL params, storage-operators CSV).
    - Extract value_range_to_datum_range for shared range conversion.
    
    Testing:
    - New pgtest copy-from-range.pt: positive COPY cases (valid ranges) and
      negative case (invalid range [7,3) expects ErrorResponse).
    
    Manual:
    &#96;&#96;&#96;
    materialize=&gt; create table t(r int4range);
    CREATE TABLE
    materialize=&gt; INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 10));
    INSERT 0 1
    materialize=&gt; INSERT INTO t VALUES (int4range(7, 3));
    ERROR:  range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound
    materialize=&gt; COPY t FROM STDIN;
    Enter data to be copied followed by a newline.
    End with a backslash and a period on a line by itself, or an EOF signal.
    &gt;&gt; (3, 5)
    &gt;&gt; COPY 1
    materialize=&gt; COPY t FROM STDIN;
    Enter data to be copied followed by a newline.
    End with a backslash and a period on a line by itself, or an EOF signal.
    &gt;&gt; (5, 3)
    &gt;&gt; ERROR:  unable to decode column: range lower bound must be less than or equal to range upper bound
    &#96;&#96;&#96;

 src/environmentd/src/http/sql.rs                |   9 [32m+[m[31m-[m
 src/environmentd/tests/pgwire.rs                |   5 [32m+[m
 src/pgcopy/src/copy.rs                          |   8 [32m+[m[31m-[m
 src/pgrepr/src/lib.rs                           |   1 [32m+[m
 src/pgrepr/src/value.rs                         | 124 [32m++++++++++++++++++[m[31m------[m
 src/pgrepr/src/value/error.rs                   |  55 [32m+++++++++++[m
 src/pgwire/src/protocol.rs                      |   9 [32m+[m[31m-[m
 src/storage-operators/src/oneshot_source/csv.rs |   7 [32m+[m[31m-[m
 test/pgtest/copy-from-range.pt                  | 110 [32m+++++++++++++++++++++[m
 9 files changed, 293 insertions(+), 35 deletions(-)

```

</tool-use>

```json
                  "content": "| &#96;WITH (&lt;with_options&gt;)&#96; | The following &#96;&lt;with_options&gt;&#96; are supported:  \\| Field \\| Value \\| Description \\| \\|-------\\|-------\\|-------------\\| \\| &#96;ASSERT NOT NULL&#96; *col_ident* \\| &#96;text&#96; \\| The column identifier for which to create a [non-null assertion](#non-null-assertions). See the [partitioning guide](/transform-data/patterns/partition-by/) for restrictions on valid values and other details. \\| \\| &#96;RETAIN HISTORY FOR&#96; *retention_period* \\| &#96;interval&#96; \\| ***Private preview.*** Duration for which Materialize retains historical data, which is useful to implement [durable subscriptions](/transform-data/patterns/durable-subscriptions/#history-retention-period). Accepts positive [interval](/sql/types/interval/) values (e.g. &#96;'1hr'&#96;). Default: &#96;1s&#96;. \\| \\| &#96;REFRESH&#96; *refresh_strategy* \\| \\| [... omitted end of long line]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6225,
                  "content": "#### Refresh on commit"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6227,
                  "content": "&lt;p style=\"font-size:14px\"&gt;&lt;b&gt;Syntax:&lt;/b&gt; &lt;code&gt;REFRESH ON COMMIT&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/p&gt;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6229,
                  "content": "Materialized views in Materialize are incrementally updated by default. This means that as soon as new data arrives in the system, any dependent materialized views are automatically and continuously updated. This behavior, known as **refresh on commit**, ensures that the view's contents are always as fresh as the underlying data."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6231,
                  "content": "**&#96;REFRESH ON COMMIT&#96; is:**"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6238,
                  "content": "With &#96;REFRESH ON COMMIT&#96;, Materialize provides low-latency, up-to-date results without requiring user-defined schedules or manual refreshes. This model is ideal for most workloads, including streaming analytics, live dashboards, customer-facing queries, and applications that rely on timely, accurate results."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6244,
                  "content": "&lt;p style=\"font-size:14px\"&gt;&lt;b&gt;Syntax:&lt;/b&gt; &lt;code&gt;REFRESH AT&lt;/code&gt; { &lt;code&gt;CREATION&lt;/code&gt; | &lt;i&gt;timestamp&lt;/i&gt; }&lt;/p&gt;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6275,
                  "content": "&lt;p style=\"font-size:14px\"&gt;&lt;b&gt;Syntax:&lt;/b&gt; &lt;code&gt;REFRESH EVERY&lt;/code&gt; &lt;i&gt;interval&lt;/i&gt; [ &lt;code&gt;ALIGNED TO&lt;/code&gt; &lt;i&gt;timestamp&lt;/i&gt; ]&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/p&gt;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 7598,
                  "content": "&lt;ul style=\"column-count: 3\"&gt;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10076,
                  "content": "the information and rendering style of the generated explanation output."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 10693,
                  "content": "| **ENVELOPE** (&#96;DEBEZIUM&#96; \\| &#96;UPSERT&#96;) | Optional. Specifies how changes to the sink's upstream relation are mapped to Kafka messages. Valid envelope types:  \\| Envelope \\| Description \\| \\|----------\\|-------------\\| \\| &#96;DEBEZIUM&#96; \\| The generated schemas have a [Debezium-style diff envelope](/sql/create-sink/kafka/#debezium) to capture changes in the input view or source. \\| \\| &#96;UPSERT&#96; \\| The sink emits data with [upsert semantics](/sql/create-sink/kafka/#upsert).                   "content": "| &#96;WITH (&lt;with_options&gt;)&#96; | The following &#96;&lt;with_options&gt;&#96; are supported:  \\| Field \\| Value \\| Description \\| \\|-------\\|-------\\|-------------\\| \\| &#96;ASSERT NOT NULL&#96; *col_ident* \\| &#96;text&#96; \\| The column identifier for which to create a [non-null assertion](#non-null-assertions). To specify multiple columns, use the option multiple times. \\| \\| &#96;PARTITION BY&#96; *columns* \\| &#96;(ident [, ident]*)&#96; \\| The key by which Materialize should internally partition this durable collection. See the [partitioning guide](/transform-data/patterns/partition-by/) for restrictions on valid values and other details. \\| \\| &#96;RETAIN HISTORY FOR&#96; *retention_period* \\| &#96;interval&#96; \\| ***Private preview.*** Duration for which Materialize retains historical data, which is useful to implement [durable subscriptions](/transform-data/patterns/durable-subscriptions/#history-retention-period). Accepts positive [interval](/sql/types/interval/) values (e.g. &#96;'1hr'&#96;). Default: &#96;1s&#96;. \\| \\| &#96;REFRESH&#96; *refresh_strategy* \\| \\| [... omitted end of long line]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 140,
                  "content": "#### Refresh on commit"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 142,
                  "content": "&lt;p style=\"font-size:14px\"&gt;&lt;b&gt;Syntax:&lt;/b&gt; &lt;code&gt;REFRESH ON COMMIT&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/p&gt;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 144,
                  "content": "Materialized views in Materialize are incrementally updated by default. This means that as soon as new data arrives in the system, any dependent materialized views are automatically and continuously updated. This behavior, known as **refresh on commit**, ensures that the view's contents are always as fresh as the underlying data."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 146,
                  "content": "**&#96;REFRESH ON COMMIT&#96; is:**"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 153,
                  "content": "With &#96;REFRESH ON COMMIT&#96;, Materialize provides low-latency, up-to-date results without requiring user-defined schedules or manual refreshes. This model is ideal for most workloads, including streaming analytics, live dashboards, customer-facing queries, and applications that rely on timely, accurate results."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 159,
                  "content": "&lt;p style=\"font-size:14px\"&gt;&lt;b&gt;Syntax:&lt;/b&gt; &lt;code&gt;REFRESH AT&lt;/code&gt; { &lt;code&gt;CREATION&lt;/code&gt; | &lt;i&gt;timestamp&lt;/i&gt; }&lt;/p&gt;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 190,
                  "content": "&lt;p style=\"font-size:14px\"&gt;&lt;b&gt;Syntax:&lt;/b&gt; &lt;code&gt;REFRESH EVERY&lt;/code&gt; &lt;i&gt;interval&lt;/i&gt; [ &lt;code&gt;ALIGNED TO&lt;/code&gt; &lt;i&gt;timestamp&lt;/i&gt; ]&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/p&gt;"
                }
              ]

                  "lineNumber": 1051,

                  "lineNumber": 1062,

                  "lineNumber": 1065,

                  "lineNumber": 1113,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 1114,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 1120,

            {
              "file": "./doc/user/content/sql/create-materialized-view.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 131,
                  "content": "#### Refresh on commit"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 133,
                  "content": "&lt;p style=\"font-size:14px\"&gt;&lt;b&gt;Syntax:&lt;/b&gt; &lt;code&gt;REFRESH ON COMMIT&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/p&gt;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 135,
                  "content": "Materialized views in Materialize are incrementally updated by default. This means that as soon as new data arrives in the system, any dependent materialized views are automatically and continuously updated. This behavior, known as **refresh on commit**, ensures that the view's contents are always as fresh as the underlying data."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 137,
                  "content": "**&#96;REFRESH ON COMMIT&#96; is:**"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 144,
                  "content": "With &#96;REFRESH ON COMMIT&#96;, Materialize provides low-latency, up-to-date results without requiring user-defined schedules or manual refreshes. This model is ideal for most workloads, including streaming analytics, live dashboards, customer-facing queries, and applications that rely on timely, accurate results."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 150,
                  "content": "&lt;p style=\"font-size:14px\"&gt;&lt;b&gt;Syntax:&lt;/b&gt; &lt;code&gt;REFRESH AT&lt;/code&gt; { &lt;code&gt;CREATION&lt;/code&gt; | &lt;i&gt;timestamp&lt;/i&gt; }&lt;/p&gt;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 181,
                  "content": "&lt;p style=\"font-size:14px\"&gt;&lt;b&gt;Syntax:&lt;/b&gt; &lt;code&gt;REFRESH EVERY&lt;/code&gt; &lt;i&gt;interval&lt;/i&gt; [ &lt;code&gt;ALIGNED TO&lt;/code&gt; &lt;i&gt;timestamp&lt;/i&gt; ]&lt;/code&gt;&lt;/p&gt;"
                }
              ]
            },

              "file": "./doc/developer/guide-changes.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 41,
                  "content": "### Commit strategy"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 48,
                  "content": "it up into commits. There are two schools of thought regarding commit hygiene."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 50,
                  "content": "  * **Semantic commits.** In the semantic commits approach, *each commit* is the"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 51,
                  "content": "    unit of review. Every commit should have a clear purpose and a well-written"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 52,
                  "content": "    commit message."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 54,
                  "content": "    If you use this approach, you simply write \"see commit messages for details\""
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 56,
                  "content": "    there are many commits involved. When you merge a PR, use the standard merge"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 57,
                  "content": "    strategy so that the semantic commits are preserved on the &#96;main&#96; branch."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 62,
                  "content": "    of review, and there is no meaning to the division of commits. It is common"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 63,
                  "content": "    to simply commit as you go with this approach, with messages like \"feature"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 67,
                  "content": "    writing a good PR description, rather than writing good commit messages."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 69,
                  "content": "    commits will be replaced with a neat, squashed commit on the &#96;main&#96; branch"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 106,
                  "content": "Writing good commit messages (or PR descriptions, if you use the semantic PRs"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 110,
                  "content": "Most Git commit conventions agree on at least the following form:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 112,
                  "content": "     Summarize the commit in the [imperative]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 118,
                  "content": "That is, the first line of a commit message is a short sentence, phrased as a"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 119,
                  "content": "command, that summarizes the contents of the commit. Then a detailed description"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 136,
                  "content": "If the commit message closes an issue, you can tell GitHub to automatically"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 141,
                  "content": "in a commit or PR description. There are several other [supported"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 144,
                  "content": "A classic example of a bad commit message is"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 156,
                  "content": "Your release note may sound very similar to your commit message. That's okay!"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 208,
                  "content": "commit message. They should complete the sentence, \"This release will...\"."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 572,
                  "content": "be productive. Therefore, they are allowed to choose whichever commit strategy"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 576,
                  "content": "the reviewer, whichever you prefer. Beware that with the semantic commit"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 577,
                  "content": "strategy, Reviewable works much better, as it can track a set of commits across"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 891,
                  "content": "The only major deviation is that we permit PRs to contain multiple commits"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 892,
                  "content": "when using the [\"semantic commits\" strategy](#commit-strategy), whereas Google's"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 893,
                  "content": "PR equivalent, the CL (\"change list\"), always contains exactly one commit."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 914,
                  "content": "* Never include refactoring and behavior changes in the same commit."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 933,

                  "lineNumber": 3,

                {
                  "lineNumber": 7,
                  "content": "For general spelling and usage, follow [Merriam-Webster.com](https://www.merriam-webster.com/). For other style questions not covered here, follow AP Style."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 30,
                  "content": "* Don’t use language that points to a specific point in time (e.g., now, new, yet, currently, in the future, soon, etc.) Refer to [Google’s guide to writing timeless documentation](https://developers.google.com/style/timeless-documentation)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 69,
                  "content": "For further information and examples, see [Google’s Cross-References guide](https://developers.google.com/style/cross-references)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 96,
                  "content": "- Adhere to AP style unless specified otherwise."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 128,
                  "content": "Use code style for code examples, keywords, values, table names, and field/column names. For example, the boolean keywords/values &#96;true&#96; and &#96;false&#96;, the string/value &#96;“foo”&#96;, the integer &#96;1234&#96;, the keyword &#96;print&#96;, the table name &#96;ALL_CAPS&#96;, and the column name &#96;lower_case&#96; should all be code-styled."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 146,
                  "content": "Use note style ( &#96;&gt;&#96; in Markdown) for notes. Do not use the &#96;{% info %}&#96; note style. How you introduce your note depends on the urgency of its content:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 166,
                  "content": "When listing data types, write their names in all caps, but *do not* put them in code style. For example, INTEGER, STRING, BINARY."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./misc/fivetran-sdk/doc-templates/style-guide/common-terms-glossary.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 5,
                  "content": "This glossary is not an extensive list of terms that may be used in the documentation. For any professional term that is not mentioned in this document, default to the industry standard. For general spelling and usage, follow [Merriam-Webster.com](https://www.merriam-webster.com/). The Merriam-Webster dictionary is the primary dictionary for the AP Style, which is the backbone of the Fivetran style."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 96,
                  "content": "**whitelist** Don't use. Use **safelist** instead. See also [Writing inclusive documentation](https://developers.google.com/style/inclusive-documentation)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 250,
                  "content": "**JOIN:** all-caps when referring to the SQL keyword, and when possible in a monotype font to indicate code style"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 348,
                  "content": "**timestamp**: one word, per coding convention (NB: this deviates from AP style)"
                }
              ]
            },

              "file": "./doc/developer/tracing.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "&gt; In asynchronous systems like Tokio, interpreting traditional log messages can often be quite challenging. Since individual tasks are multiplexed on the same thread, associated events and log lines are intermixed making it difficult to trace the logic flow. tracing expands upon logging-style diagnostics by allowing libraries and applications to record structured events with additional information about temporality and causality — unlike a log message, a span in tracing has a beginning and end time, may be entered and exited by the flow of execution, and may exist within a nested tree of similar spans. In addition, tracing spans are structured, with the ability to record typed data as well as textual messages."
                }
              ]
            },

              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 1,
                  "content": "# Style guide"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6,
                  "content": "## SQL style"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 8,
                  "content": "The PostgreSQL manual adheres to a consistent style for its SQL statements, and"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 95,
                  "content": "### Error message style"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 98,
                  "content": "For error message style, we strive to adhere to the [guidelines](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/error-style-guide.html) in the PostgreSQL manual, and use a format that consists of a **required** _primary_ message followed by **optional** _detail_ and _hint_ messages."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 112,
                  "content": "To use a shortlink in a Hint, add the shortlink to this [file](https://github.com/MaterializeInc/materialize/blob/3efb2c933e4e6c8e694afb1e794c7d8dae368e7d/ci/deploy/website.sh#L29) in your PR to add/modify the hint message. Be mindful that we are committed to the upkeep of the referenced link."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 125,
                  "content": "### System catalog style"
                },

                  "content": "This is the default when not supplying any options, i.e., our current behavior of refreshing the materialized view when any changes to our inputs are committed. Automated replica management is not implemented yet, but one can manually drop and create replicas."
                },

                  "lineNumber": 267,
                  "content": "For showing the &#96;REFRESH&#96; options of each materialized view, I'll create a new table in &#96;mz_internal&#96; called &#96;mz_materialized_view_refresh_strategies&#96;, which will have a row for each non-trivial refresh option of each MV. The columns will be &#96;(materialized_view_id text, type text, interval interval, aligned_to timestamptz, at timestamptz)&#96;. In &#96;type&#96; we would show either one of the strings \"every\", \"at\", or \"on-commit\". From those columns that show details of a particular refresh option, only those columns will have a non-null value that are relevant for the refresh option that the row is about: a &#96;REFRESH EVERY&#96; would be non-null in &#96;interval&#96; and &#96;aligned_to&#96;, a &#96;REFRESH AT&#96; would be non-null in &#96;at&#96;.                 },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "Many GUI and other tools however always wrap their statements with &#96;BEGIN&#96; and &#96;COMMIT&#96;,"

                  "lineNumber": 17,
                  "content": "  as long as there is exactly one statement in the transaction: &#96;BEGIN; &lt;stmt&gt;; COMMIT&#96;."

                  "lineNumber": 30,
                  "content": "On &#96;COMMIT&#96;, the statement executes, returning any errors as normal."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 40,
                  "content": "On &#96;COMMIT&#96;, the coordinator clears the current transaction, fetching and processing its inner operation (this already occurs before this document)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 41,
                  "content": "If the inner operation is &#96;SingleStatement&#96;, the transaction status is set to &#96;Started&#96; (a status that can execute any statement), the recorded statement is executed, and an implicit &#96;COMMIT&#96; is executed."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 42,
                  "content": "The implicit &#96;COMMIT&#96; puts the transaction into the same final state (&#96;Default&#96;) as the explicit &#96;COMMIT&#96;."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 60,
                  "content": "In order for a session to execute a statement, the protocol handler must set this to one of the other states then end the transaction with a commit or rollback."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 61,
                  "content": "&#96;Started&#96; is used for a single statement, so an immediate and implied commit follows."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 62,
                  "content": "&#96;InTransaction&#96; is used when a user explicitly types &#96;BEGIN&#96;, and can only be exited when a user sends &#96;COMMIT&#96; or &#96;ROLLBACK&#96;."

                {
                  "lineNumber": 63,

                  "lineNumber": 71,
```

```
    [10057] [Planner] Treat empty int2vector as 1 dimensional (#34981)
    
    ## Problem:
    Panics arise in the execution/optimization with empty int2vector's
    &#96;&#96;&#96;
    ERROR:  internal error: internal error in optimizer: internal transform error
    int2vector dimensions differ: got datum with dimension 0, expected dimension 1
    &#96;&#96;&#96;
    
    ## Solution:
    Introduce has_int2vector_dims which validates either the array is
    1-dimensional, or 0 dimensional, and empty. Use this in 3 places where
    checked
    
    ## Testing:
    New tests in int2vector.slt that test empty int2vector's, or rows with
    empty vector's
    
    ### Motivation
    
    https://github.com/MaterializeInc/database-issues/issues/10057
```

**User's style (from f79fc1c and 99e6811):**
1. **Subject line:** `[<issue-number>] [<Area>] <Imperative summary>`

   - e.g. `[10057] [Planner] Treat empty int2vector as 1 dimensional (#34981)` (PR number in parens when merged)
2. **Body sections:** Problem / Solution / Testing (and optionally Manual)
   - **Problem:** What was wrong (user-visible symptom, why it mattered).
   - **Solution:** Bullet list of what was done (code-level changes).
   - **Testing:** What tests were added/run (e.g. new pgtest, slt tests).
   - **Manual:** (optional) Copy-pastable session showing the fix behavior in a fenced code block.

**Materialize style guide (guide-changes.md):**
- First line: short sentence, imperative mood, summarizes the commit.
- Detailed description after blank line: rationale, alternatives, future work; wrap at 72 chars; blank lines between paragraphs.
- Good example given: `coord: add integer_datetimes server var` (so they also use `area: summary` sometimes, but the user uses `[Area]` in brackets).
- Bad example: "Fix bug." (too vague).

```
[33mcommit a1ec59f66589c0d162e530f4f674112b1f5c4152[m
Author: mtabebe &lt;michael.abebe@materialize.com&gt;
Commit: GitHub &lt;noreply@github.com&gt;

    10006 [Planner] Add error in planning for unsupported range types (#34926)
    
    ## Problem:
    Using an unsupported type with ranges (e.g., float) leads to a panic in
    either the optimizer or execution:
    &#96;&#96;&#96;
    ERROR: internal error: internal error in optimizer: internal transform error: unexpected panic during query optimization: internal error: entered unreachable code: UInt16(1) not yet supported in ranges
    &#96;&#96;&#96;
    
    ## Solution:
    Introduce a new error in get_cast that validates the range type
    (validate_range_element_type) to only the allowed range types (int32,
    int64, date, numeric, timestamp:
    https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/rangetypes.html)
    
    Introduce this in get_cast because this is used in the planning phase.
    If we simply introduce an error in the actual evaluation (e.g., in
    RangeBound::canonicalize) then it is possible that if the table is
    empty, an error is not thrown
    
    ## Testing:
    New tests in range.slt that tests ranges with uint2 and floats.
    
    ### Motivation
    
      * This PR fixes a recognized bug.
    
    https://github.com/MaterializeInc/database-issues/issues/10065

```

Here's what’s in place.

**Skill:** `commit-messages-materialize`  
**Location:** `~/.cursor/skills/commit-messages-materialize/SKILL.md` (personal, so it applies in any project where you want this style).

**What it does:**
- **Subject:** `[<issue>] [<Area>] <Imperative summary>` (e.g. `[10089] [Adapter] Fix COPY panic on invalid range by making into_datum return Result`).
- **Body:** Problem → Solution (bullets) → Testing → Manual (optional), matching your f79fc1c structure.
- **Aligns with** `doc/developer/guide-changes.md`: imperative first line, 72-char wrap, specific (no “Fix bug”), and the referenced example commit.

**When it’s used:** When you ask for a commit message, to summarize changes for a commit, or to draft a squashed PR commit message; the description tells the agent to use it for “commit message,” “my commit style,” or “Materialize commits.”

**In practice:** Ask things like “Write a commit message for my staged changes” or “Summarize this diff as a commit in my style” and the agent will use this skill to produce messages in that format. You can tweak the template or add more examples by editing `~/.cursor/skills/commit-messages-materialize/SKILL.md`.

Summary of what’s in place:

**Skill:** `materialize-testing`  
**Path:** `.cursor/skills/materialize-testing/SKILL.md` (project skill in the Materialize repo)

**Contents:**

1. **Where to put tests** — Table mapping “what you’re testing” to location and format (sqllogictest in `test/sqllogictest/`, testdrive in `test/testdrive/`, pgtest in `test/pgtest/`, unit tests next to code, and when to add a new `test/` dir with mzcompose).

2. **Running tests**
   - **Rust:** `bin/cargo-test`, `cargo test -p CRATE`, `--nocapture`, `MZ_TEST_LOG_FILTER`; pgtest via `cargo test -p environmentd pgtest` and that new `.pt` files need a test in `environmentd/tests/pgwire.rs`.
   - **sqllogictest:** `bin/sqllogictest [--optimized] -- test/sqllogictest/FILE.slt`, `-vv`, `--rewrite-results`.
   - **testdrive:** `cd test/testdrive && ./mzcompose run default` (or `default FILE.td`, `--redpanda`), plus the two-terminal local run with `bin/environmentd` + `cargo run --bin testdrive -- test/testdrive/FILE.td`.
   - **Other workflows:** `cd test/<dir> && ./mzcompose down -v && ./mzcompose run default`.

3. **testdrive syntax** — Short reference for `>`, `!`, `$ set`, `$ kafka-ingest`, `$ set-regex`, `? EXPLAIN`, multi-line SQL, and lint rules (copyright, no trailing spaces, `kafka-*.td`).

4. **sqllogictest** — Short reference for `query I rowsort`, `statement`/`query error`, `----`, `mode cockroach`/`standard`, `reset-server`, `simple`, and testing NULLs/edges.

5. **Reproducing bugs** — When to use .slt vs .pt vs .td vs Rust tests and how to run them.

6. **Checklist** — Quick check before adding a test (framework, location, lint, pgtest wiring, run locally).

The description uses the trigger phrases you asked for (“Add a test”, “Run testdrive”, “Reproduce this bug”, “Test this SQL”) so the agent will apply this skill in those cases. Deeper details are left to the linked docs (guide-testing, testdrive, sqllogictest, mzcompose) to keep the skill under 500 lines.
